title: Beef and Barley Stew
subtitle: Slow-cooked beef shin with pearl barley and root vegetables in a thick, hearty broth
excerpt: Beef and barley stew is a hob or oven braise of beef shin with pearl barley and root vegetables. The barley thickens the stock naturally as it cooks and adds a soft, nutty grain to each bowlful. It takes 2.5 hours and produces a stew that is richer and more filling than any recipe with fewer ingredients deserves to be.
sourceNotes: Traditional British and Scottish. F. Marian McNeill, The Scots Kitchen (1929): Scotch broth with barley and lamb. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: barley broth. British farmhouse cooking tradition.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze for up to 3 months. The barley will soften further on reheating; this is not a problem. Reheat gently with a splash of stock to loosen.
recipe.batchNotes: This recipe doubles easily in a large stockpot. Make a large batch on the weekend and refrigerate for up to 4 days.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The stew improves overnight. Make a day ahead and reheat slowly over low heat.
recipe.temperatureNote: fan oven if baking; or simmer on the hob with the lid on

glossaryTerms.definition: Hulled barley with the outer bran layer removed, leaving a smooth, round white grain. It absorbs liquid during cooking and thickens stew naturally through starch release. Unlike pasta, pearl barley continues to absorb stock even after the heat is off; add it with at least 40 minutes of cooking time remaining so it is cooked through but not collapsed.

The lower leg of the animal, a hard-working muscle that is dense with collagen. After 2 to 2.5 hours of slow cooking, the collagen dissolves into the cooking liquid, giving the broth a rich, silky body. Beef shin is inexpensive, holds its texture well during long braising, and produces a deeper result than dearer cuts.

content.text: The key to this stew is

: its high collagen content gives the broth a body that no amount of stock cube can replicate. Braising steak or beef chuck will produce a good stew, but the texture of the finished liquid with shin is noticeably richer.

after 90 minutes of cooking, not at the start. Added too early, it absorbs too much liquid and the stew becomes claggy. Added at the right time, it swells to a soft, nutty grain and thickens the broth just enough.

items.prepNote: cut into large chunks, or use beef chuck

content.text: Brown the beef and build the base
in a large casserole over high heat. Season the
. Brown in batches for 3-4 minutes per side until deeply coloured. Remove and set aside. Reduce heat to medium. Add the onion, carrot, and celery and cook for 8 minutes. Add garlic,
. Bring to a simmer, cover, and cook on the lowest hob heat (or in a 160°C oven) for 90 minutes.

. Check the liquid level; add a splash of water or stock if the pot is looking dry. Replace the lid and cook for a further 40-50 minutes until the beef is completely tender and the barley is soft. Season with
. Remove the thyme sprigs and bay leaves. Serve with flat-leaf parsley if using.

rows.symptom: Stew is too thick and the barley has absorbed all the stock
rows.cause: Barley added too early, or pot was left on too high a heat
rows.fix: Add hot stock or water in small amounts to reach the consistency you want. Barley keeps absorbing liquid; serve the stew as soon as it is ready.

rows.cause: The heat was too high, causing the muscle fibres to tighten rather than relax
rows.fix: Reduce the heat to the barest simmer. Beef shin requires low, steady heat; boiling makes it tough rather than tender.

rows.symptom: Broth is thin and lacks body
rows.cause: Too much stock for the volume of collagen in the meat
rows.fix: Remove the lid and simmer uncovered for 15-20 minutes to reduce. If still thin, the beef may not have been high enough in collagen; next time use more shin rather than chuck.

rows.cause: Added at the start and cooked for too long
rows.fix: For a tidier result, add fresh diced carrot and celery at the same time as the barley so they cook for only 40 minutes.

content.text: Beef and barley stew sits alongside Scotch broth as one of the defining British grain-thickened soups. The Scottish version traditionally uses lamb or mutton with barley, leek, and turnip; the English version more often uses beef and is served as a thick stew rather than a broth. Both are made to feed a large group from inexpensive cuts and last well in the refrigerator.
